Running it needs command approval, which isn't granted in this session ‚Äî so rather than keep retrying, here's the optimized version plus an analytical speedup (and I'll happily run a measured benchmark if you approve `node`).

## Optimized

```javascript
function isPrime(n) {
  if (n < 2) return false;
  if (n < 4) return true;          // 2 and 3
  if (n % 2 === 0) return false;   // drop all evens
  for (let i = 3; i * i <= n; i += 2)   // only odd divisors, up to ‚àön
    if (n % i === 0) return false;
  return true;
}
```

Behaviorally identical to yours (same true/false for every integer, including the `n > 1` edge cases), just doing far less work.

## How much faster

Two independent wins:

- **`i * i <= n` instead of `i < n`** ‚Äî stops at ‚àön. If a number has a factor, it has one ‚â§ ‚àön, so everything past that is wasted. This is the big one: **O(n) ‚Üí O(‚àön)**.
- **`i += 2` after handling 2** ‚Äî skips even divisors, halving the remaining iterations.

The speedup isn't a single number ‚Äî it grows with `n`. For a prime near `n` (the worst case, where the loop runs to completion), the old loop does ~`n` iterations and the new one ~`‚àön / 2`, so:

$$\text{speedup} \approx \frac{n}{\sqrt{n}/2} = 2\sqrt{n}$$

| `n` (prime) | old iters | new iters | ~speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1,000 | 1,000 | ~16 | ~60√ó |
| 1,000,003 | ~1,000,000 | ~500 | ~2,000√ó |
| 1,000,000,007 | ~1,000,000,000 | ~15,800 | ~63,000√ó |

For composites both versions bail early at the smallest factor, so the dramatic gains are on primes and large numbers ‚Äî exactly the cases that were slow before.

If you want an empirical figure on your machine, approve a `node` run and I'll benchmark both over a real range (I had a correctness-checked harness ready ‚Äî it confirms identical output for all `n` before timing).

One note: if you need this for genuinely large `n` (cryptographic sizes), trial division stops being viable entirely and you'd want **Miller‚ÄìRabin** instead. Say the word and I'll drop one in.
